title: Roast Grouse
subtitle: The Glorious Twelfth bird: roasted briefly, served on toast spread with the liver
excerpt: Grouse season opens on 12 August. A young bird roasts in 20-25 minutes at very high heat and needs nothing more than butter, bacon, and the traditional toast spread with the bird's own liver. The flavour is intense, heathery, and unlike any other game bird.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: roasting grouse with toast. Jane Grigson, English Food (1974): grouse on toast with liver butter. Traditional British game season practice.

recipe.temperatureNote: very high heat for the initial roast; rest at room temperature

glossaryTerms.definition: The traditional opening day of the grouse shooting season in Britain, 12 August. Red grouse are native to British and Irish heather moorland and cannot be farmed. The earliest birds of the season, eaten within days of the shoot, are the most prized.
Toast spread with the bird's own liver, quickly fried in butter, then mashed and seasoned. Traditionally placed under the resting grouse, or served alongside as a first taste before the carved bird.

content.text: Grouse is the most distinctive of the British game birds. The flavour comes from the heather the birds eat on Scottish and northern English moors, and it cannot be replicated. A young bird from the early weeks of the season is mild enough to eat slightly underdone, with the breast just rosy at the centre. An older bird, or one from later in the season, is stronger and benefits from hanging a little longer.
The method is minimal. The bird is barded with bacon, roasted at very high heat, and rested on toast spread with the
made from the bird's own liver. The
tradition demands that the first grouse of the season reach London restaurants the same day it is shot.

items.prepNote: grouse, approx. 450-500 g each, one per person, giblets reserved
divided: 30 g for the birds, 20 g for the liver toast

content.text: Remove the livers from the giblets and pat dry. Melt 20 g of the
in a small frying pan over high heat. Fry the livers for 2 minutes until firm outside but still pink within. Tip onto a board and chop roughly, then mash with a fork into a paste. Season with

Heat the oven to 230°C (210°C fan). Pat the birds dry and rub with the remaining butter. Tuck thyme into each cavity. Lay bacon across each breast. Roast for 20 minutes, remove the bacon, then roast for 5 more minutes to colour the breast skin. A probe should read 70-72°C in the thigh for a slightly pink result; 75°C for fully cooked through. Rest for 10 minutes.
Spread the liver paste thickly onto the toast slices. Set a rested grouse on each toast. Add
to the roasting pan, scrape the fond, and strain the gravy into a warm jug. Carve at the table: each diner has the whole bird on toast, with the gravy alongside.

rows.cause: Bacon removed too early or oven not fully preheated to 230°C
rows.fix: Remove bacon only at 20 minutes. Grouse needs the full heat of a properly preheated oven.

rows.cause: Livers were overcooked or gall bladder was broken, releasing bile
rows.fix: Fry livers briefly, keeping them pink inside. If bitter, discard and serve without the toast; watercress is an acceptable substitute.

rows.symptom: Bird tastes too strong for the occasion
rows.fix: Early-season birds (August-September) are milder. Ask your game dealer for young brace from the early days of the season.

rows.symptom: Toast goes soggy under the resting bird
rows.cause: Normal; the juices soak into the bread
rows.fix: This is intentional. The toast absorbs the resting juices and liver paste and is part of the dish.

content.text: Grouse on toast is the dish that British food writers return to every August. The bird is native, tied to a specific landscape, and brief in its season. To eat a grouse roasted this simply is to taste something that cannot be replicated outside the heather moorlands of Scotland, northern England, and Ireland. The method is almost unchanged from Victorian tables.
